title: 20-Year Term Life Insurance Canada (2026)
seo_title: 20-Year Term Life Insurance Canada | Compare Rates | PolicyScanner
seo_description: Get 20-year term life insurance in Canada from $25/month. Compare rates from 20+ top insurers. Ideal for homeowners and families. Free quotes.

_meta.page_role: Core product page — the most-searched term life product. Maximum depth + conversion.

seo_keywords.density_rule: Use '20-year term' or '20-year term life insurance' 4–6 times per 500 words. Vary with synonyms below.
seo_keywords.real_example_to_use: Michael, 35, bought $750,000 of 20-year term for $42/month — covers his $600K mortgage and family during children's school years.
seo_keywords.avoid: Do not say '20-year term life insurance in Canada' as a visible keyword phrase — structure naturally: 'Canadians who buy 20-year term...' or 'a 20-year term in Canada...'

_writer_notes.focus: Most popular term length — validate their choice immediately
key_points: State it's the #1 most purchased term life insurance in Canada
Perfect match for mortgages (20-25 year amortization) + young family years
Starting price: from $25/month for $500K coverage
Breadcrumb: Home > Life Insurance > Term Life Insurance > 20-Year Term

_writer_notes.focus: Simple definition + why 20 years specifically makes sense
key_points: DEFINITION: Fixed coverage for exactly 20 years. If you die during the 20-year term, your beneficiaries receive the tax-free death benefit in full.
PREMIUMS: Fixed for the entire 20 years — they never increase during the term.
COVERAGE AMOUNTS typically range from $100,000 to $5,000,000+
WHY 20 YEARS: Matches the most common mortgage amortization period (20-25 years). Covers the full child-rearing phase (birth to adulthood). Locks in your lowest rates during your peak earning years.
WHAT HAPPENS AT END: Coverage expires. Options: renew (at higher rate), convert to permanent, or buy new policy.

CALLOUT: 'The 20-year term is Canada's most popular life insurance product — and for good reason. It matches the two most common financial obligations: your mortgage and your children's dependent years.'

page_data.title: Why 20 Years is Canada's Most Popular Term
page_data.text: The 20-year term matches Canada's two biggest financial obligations perfectly: the average mortgage amortization (20–25 years) and the child-rearing phase (birth to adulthood). It locks in affordable rates during your peak earning years.

page_data.heading: Who Should Buy 20-Year Term Life Insurance?

_writer_notes.focus: Help the reader self-identify — make them feel 'yes, that's clearly me'
key_points: PRIMARY: New homeowners with 20-25 year mortgages — coverage runs alongside your mortgage
PRIMARY: Parents of young children — coverage lasts until children are financially independent (ages 20+)
AGES 30–50: Peak years to buy — best balance of need and affordability
INCOME EARNERS: Replace your income for 20 years if you die unexpectedly — your family can maintain their standard of living
DUAL-INCOME FAMILIES: Even if partner works, losing one income is devastating — protect both incomes
BUSINESS OWNERS: Cover business loans, key-person risk, or buy-sell agreement obligations
MINI SCENARIOS (write each as 2-3 sentences):
1. Young family: Sarah, 32, new mom, $600K mortgage — 20-year term runs until kids are grown and mortgage is nearly paid
2. New homeowner: Ahmed, 38, just bought his first home — 20-year term covers the mortgage precisely
3. Single income family: The Liu family relies on David's $85K salary — 20-year term ensures 20 years of income replacement

page_data.heading: How Much Does 20-Year Term Life Insurance Cost?
paragraphs: 20-year term life insurance is more affordable than most people expect. Here are real sample rates from top Canadian insurers for 2026.

_writer_notes.focus: Lead with shocking affordability — most people overestimate cost by 3x
key_points: Write 2-3 sentences before each table introducing what it shows
STAT: '72% of Canadians overestimate the cost of life insurance by more than 3 times'
FACTORS after tables: Age (biggest factor), Gender (women 20-30% less), Smoking (2-4x more), Health, Coverage amount
TIP: 'Buy as young as possible — locking in at 30 vs 40 can save you $30–50/month for 20 years'

page_data.title: $500,000 — 20-Year Term Monthly Premiums
page_data.footnote: Sample rates from top Canadian insurers, 2026. Non-smoker = nicotine-free for 12+ months. Actual premium depends on health history and underwriting.

page_data.title: $1,000,000 — 20-Year Term Monthly Premiums
page_data.footnote: $1M coverage is common for dual-income households, business owners, and high-income earners. Most insurers offer $1M+ without requiring additional medical exam steps below age 40.

page_data.title: Lock In Your Rate While You're Young
page_data.text: Life insurance premiums roughly double every 10 years as you age. Buying a 20-year term at age 30 vs age 40 can save you $30–50/month — that's $7,200–$12,000 over the term of the policy.

page_data.title: Calculate Your 20-Year Term Life Insurance Cost

page_data.heading: How Much 20-Year Term Coverage Do You Need?

_writer_notes.focus: Give a simple, actionable formula they can calculate in 2 minutes
key_points: RULE OF THUMB: 10–15x annual income
DIME METHOD (brief): D = Debt, I = Income replacement, M = Mortgage balance, E = Education costs for children
EXAMPLES BY SCENARIO:
Mortgage of $500K + $75K income + 2 kids = approximately $1.2M–$1.5M coverage recommended
Single income $60K + $400K mortgage = $800K–$1M coverage
Dual income, no kids, $450K mortgage = $500K–$750K each
CRITICAL: Don't just cover the mortgage — include income replacement and education costs
Link to: How Much Life Insurance Do I Need (calculator page)

_writer_notes.focus: Why the best company varies person to person — reinforce the value of comparing
key_points: Write a 2-sentence intro: rates vary significantly between companies for the same coverage
After bullets: 'The best company for a 30-year-old healthy male may not be the best for a 45-year-old female with controlled diabetes. This is why comparing matters.'
CTA: Use our comparison tool to find YOUR best rate

page_data.heading: What Happens When Your 20-Year Term Ends?

_writer_notes.focus: Answer this proactively — it's the most common 'what if' question
key_points: OPTION 1 — Let it expire: If you no longer need coverage (mortgage paid, kids grown, savings built up), simply let the policy end.
OPTION 2 — Renew: No medical exam required. Premium jumps to rate for your current age. Example: $40/month at 35 becomes $180–240/month at 55.
OPTION 3 — Convert to permanent: No medical exam. Premium increases to whole life rates. Deadline usually age 70. Best if health has declined.
OPTION 4 — Buy new term: Fresh application, new medical exam, premium based on current age. Best if still healthy.
CALLOUT: 'Best practice: If you still need coverage at the end of your term, start the new application 12–18 months early to avoid any coverage gap.'

_writer_notes.focus: Help people who are torn between term lengths make a confident decision
key_points: Write a paragraph introducing the comparison
After table: CHOOSE 10-YEAR IF: you're already in your 40s-50s with nearly paid-off mortgage, have shorter financial obligations, want absolute lowest premium
CHOOSE 20-YEAR IF: you have a mortgage with 15-25 years left, children under age 10, ages 30-45 — this is usually the right answer
CHOOSE 30-YEAR IF: you have very young children (newborns), just signed a 30-year mortgage, are in your late 20s and want to lock in rates for maximum period

page_data.title: 10 vs 20 vs 30-Year Term: Which is Right for You?
page_data.footnote: All scenarios assume male, non-smoker, good health. Female rates are approximately 20–30% lower.

page_data.heading: Get Your Free 20-Year Term Life Insurance Quote
page_data.body: Compare 20-year term rates from Canada's top insurers. Free, no obligation. Most people get their rate in under 2 minutes.

items.question: Is 20-year term life insurance the right choice for most Canadians?
items.answer: For most Canadians aged 28–45 with a mortgage and/or young children, yes. The 20-year term matches the two most common financial obligations — a mortgage and the child-rearing years — at the most affordable price point among longer-term options.

items.question: Can I cancel a 20-year term policy early?
items.answer: Yes, you can cancel anytime with no penalty. Term life insurance has no cash value, so you won't receive any money back. Simply stop paying and the policy will lapse after the grace period.

items.question: What if I develop health problems during my 20-year term?
items.answer: Your coverage continues at the same locked-in premium regardless of any health changes during the term. This is the key advantage of buying young and healthy — your rate is guaranteed for the full 20 years.

items.question: Can I get $2 million in 20-year term coverage?
items.answer: Yes. Most major Canadian insurers offer 20-year terms up to $5M or more. Coverage over $1M typically requires a full medical exam. Coverage under $500K may be available without a medical exam depending on your age and health.

items.question: How quickly can I get approved for 20-year term life insurance?
items.answer: Simplified issue (under $500K, limited health questions): 24–48 hours. Accelerated underwriting: 1–2 weeks. Full underwriting with medical exam: 4–6 weeks.

items.question: Do I need a medical exam for 20-year term life insurance?
items.answer: Depends on your age and coverage amount. Under age 40 with less than $500K coverage: usually no exam needed. Over $500K or over age 50: a paramedical exam (done at your home or office) is typically required.

items.question: Can I convert my 20-year term to permanent life insurance?
items.answer: Yes. Most 20-year term policies include a conversion option that allows you to convert to whole life or universal life insurance without a new medical exam, usually before age 70. Your premium will increase to permanent life rates at your current age.
